FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years, longitudinal to Year 2 (N=10,317)

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone as outcome variable with no separately-measured smartphone or social media exposure variable (B3)

FT_IC3.evidence: Does not meet IC2; no SM/smartphone ↔ non-SM inferential test

FT_IC4.evidence: Does not meet IC2; AORs reported for ACE → cyberbullying relationship, not SM ↔ non-SM

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with logistic regression (Table 2-3, AOR with 95% CIs)

FT_IC6.evidence: Original peer-reviewed publication, not a preprint/abstract/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet IC2 (cyberbullying outcome with no SM exposure), so EC7 criteria not evaluated. Additionally, ACEs (parental psychopathology, family conflict, etc.) are disqualifying predictors per B17.
notes: EXCLUDE: Paper addresses ACE → cyberbullying associations but does not meet FT_IC2 (cyberbullying measured as outcome, not SM/smartphone exposure as predictor of non-SM outcome).

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14 years, N=9,443

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization/perpetration alone as exposure; no separately-measured smartphone or social media variable predicting external outcome (B3)

FT_IC3.evidence: Does not meet IC2; modified Poisson regression examines cyberbullying → sleep disturbance, not SM/smartphone ↔ non-SM outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: Does not meet IC2; RRs reported for cyberbullying → sleep (RR 1.87, 95% CI 1.57-2.21), not SM ↔ non-SM

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis, cross-sectional Year 2 data with modified Poisson regression (Table 3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Original peer-reviewed publication, not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet IC2 (cyberbullying as exposure, sleep as outcome; no SM/smartphone as IV with non-SM outcome). Additionally, sleep is a disqualifying predictor per B17.
notes: EXCLUDE: Paper examines cyberbullying → sleep disturbance but does not meet FT_IC2. Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured SM/smartphone variable does not qualify.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14 years, N=10,339 (48.7% female, 46.0% racial/ethnic minority)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying smartphone/SM variables as outcomes measured separately with own coefficients: problematic social media use, problematic video games, problematic mobile phone use, video chat, texting, YouTube, social media time, television. Sexual orientation as predictor (B1)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses (Methods: Multiple linear regression analyses estimated the association between sexual orientation and recreational screen time, problematic use of video games, social media, and mobile phones). β coefficients reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients extracted: sexual minority identification 3.72 more hours daily recreational screen time (95% CI 2.96-4.47); possible sexual minority 1.58 hours (95% CI 0.92-2.24). Results for problematic social media, video games, mobile phone use reported with separate associations.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with cross-sectional data from Year 2; multiple linear regression with β coefficients and CIs

FT_IC6.evidence: Original peer-reviewed publication in Annals of Epidemiology, not a duplicate/preprint

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply. Sexual orientation is a disqualifying predictor per B17 (NOT EC7: sexual orientation, gender identity listed as predictors that disqualify FT_EC7). Cross-sectional sociodemographic/identity analysis → SM (B19) is INCLUDE but NOT EC7.
notes: INCLUDE. Meets all FT_IC1-6. FT_EC7 does not apply because sexual orientation is an explicitly disqualifying predictor per B17. This is a cross-sectional sociodemographic/identity → SM analysis per B19.
